sessionTitle: Partnering with Families of Neurodivergent Students: Building Trust, Consistency, and Collaboration
shortDescription:
Effective support for neurodivergent students depends on strong, trusting partnerships between schools and families. Yet these relationships are often strained by stigma, inconsistent communication, and systems that position families as passive recipients rather than active collaborators.

This session explores practical, justice-centered strategies for building authentic school–home partnerships with families of neurodivergent learners (including autistic, ADHD, dyslexic, and other learning differences). Participants will examine how bias and deficit-based language can unintentionally erode trust and learn concrete approaches to shift toward strengths-based, culturally responsive collaboration.

Educators will leave with immediately usable tools for communication, problem-solving, and shared decision-making that honor family expertise, center student identity, and promote consistency between home and school supports.
extendedDescription:
This session, “Partnering with Families of Neurodivergent Students: Building Trust, Consistency, and Collaboration,” explores how schools can strengthen equitable and inclusive practices by transforming the way they engage with families of neurodivergent learners. Drawing on my experience as an HR professional specializing in inclusive recruitment, alongside my lived experience as a parent of neurodivergent children, I bring a dual perspective on how systems either build trust—or unintentionally erode it. While my work is rooted in workplace inclusion, the same principles of psychological safety, communication equity, and strengths-based collaboration apply directly to education systems.

At the core of this session is the idea that justice-centered education requires more than instructional strategies; it requires relational systems built on trust, dignity, and shared expertise. Families of neurodivergent students often navigate repeated experiences of misunderstanding, deficit-based labeling, and inconsistent communication across school systems. These experiences can create barriers to collaboration and limit student success. This session reframes family engagement as a core equity practice rather than an ancillary responsibility.

The session is structured into four key components. First, participants will examine how bias, language, and system design shape family-school relationships, often unintentionally positioning educators as “experts” while minimizing family knowledge. Second, we will explore the lived experience of families navigating neurodiversity across both home and institutional systems, highlighting where communication breakdowns commonly occur and why trust can be difficult to establish.

Third, the session will focus on practical, actionable strategies for building stronger partnerships. Participants will learn strengths-based communication techniques, including how to reframe challenging conversations, reduce stigma in written and verbal communication, and create consistency between home and school supports. We will also explore structured collaboration tools, such as shared problem-solving frameworks and predictable communication routines that support transparency and follow-through.

Finally, participants will engage in application-based reflection to identify one or two immediate changes they can implement in their own context. These takeaways are designed to be realistic and sustainable, supporting educators in making meaningful shifts without adding significant burden to their workload.

This session directly aligns with the theme “Justice-Centered Education for a Changing World” by addressing the systemic and relational conditions that impact student belonging and access. In a rapidly changing educational landscape, equity cannot be achieved through instruction alone; it must also be embedded in how schools communicate, collaborate, and build trust with families. By centering neurodivergent learners and their caregivers as essential partners, this session supports educators in creating environments where all students are seen, understood, and supported.

Ultimately, this work helps educators move from compliance-driven family engagement toward authentic partnership rooted in equity, respect, and shared responsibility for student success.

sessionTitle: The Roots of Connection: Pruning for Justice-Centered Leadership
shortDescription: Justice-centered education requires more than policy shifts; it requires a transformation of leadership culture. This interactive workshop introduces the "Kathairo" principle—the intentional pruning of systemic and relational obstacles to make space for equity. Drawing on the Intercultural Development Inventory (IDI) and Harvard-certified leadership strategies, participants will learn how to prioritize Emotional and Cultural Intelligence over technical skill to build high-trust, inclusive environments where every learner and educator can flourish.
extendedDescription:
In a "Changing World," the most critical system in any school is the human one. While educational leaders are often trained to be masters of intellect and technical skill (IQ), true justice-centered transformation requires a mastery of Emotional Intelligence (EQ) and relational health. This session is designed for leaders who recognize that equitable outcomes for students cannot be achieved within a toxic or culturally stagnant adult culture.

The Kathairo Framework
"Kathairo" is the Greek concept of pruning to allow for greater fruitfulness. In the context of justice-centered education, this means identifying and removing the "dead wood" of unconscious bias, transactional management, and systemic blind spots. By pruning these obstacles, leaders create the necessary room for inclusive, justice-oriented practices to take root and grow.

Session Structure and Approach
This 90-minute interactive workshop moves beyond theory into practical, systems-level application. The session is divided into three "Pruning Phases":

The Intercultural Audit (30 mins): Utilizing the framework of the Intercultural Development Inventory (IDI), we will analyze how "Cultural Confusion" stalls justice initiatives. Participants will engage in a self-assessment exercise to identify their current leadership posture along the intercultural development continuum.

Relational vs. Transactional Systems (30 mins): We will explore why IQ-heavy leadership often fails in diverse settings. Through small-group case studies, participants will practice "Relational Leadership" techniques; prioritizing empathy and psychological safety as the foundation for systemic equity.

The Systems-Change Blueprint (30 mins): Participants will work in collaborative "design pods" to apply the Kathairo principle to a specific challenge in their home organization (e.g., hiring practices, disciplinary equity, or staff retention). They will leave with a 30-day "Pruning Plan" to initiate immediate cultural shifts.

Alignment with Justice-Centered Education
Justice-centered education requires leaders to act as bridge-builders. By focusing on the "Architecture of Connection," this session equips educators to dismantle the silos that separate us. We move toward a model where equity is not a "program" we implement, but a natural byproduct of a healthy, high-EQ organizational culture.
takeaways: Conduct a Relational Audit: Participants will be able to identify "systemic dead wood"—specific relational and cultural habits within their organizations that hinder justice and equity initiatives.
Implement EQ-Driven Leadership: Leaders will gain 3 practical "Relational ROI" templates to facilitate high-trust
cross-cultural conversations with staff and community stakeholders.
Apply Intercultural Competence: Using the IDI framework
participants will be able to move their teams from "minimizing" cultural differences to "adapting" to them
creating a more inclusive environment for diverse learners.
Execute a 30-Day Pruning Plan: Participants will leave with a documented action plan to shift one specific organizational system from a transactional model to a justice-centered
relational model.

sessionTitle: Check Your BS®: When Comfort Gets in the Way of Equity
shortDescription: Equity requires more than awareness. It requires action, especially in uncomfortable moments. This session uses the Check Your BS® (Belief System) transformational coaching framework and Energy Leadership™ to examine how comfort, avoidance, and unchallenged beliefs show up in education. Participants will learn how to recognize internal barriers and make more intentional choices that support equitable, inclusive learning environments.
extendedDescription:
Let's be clear. Equity requires more than awareness. 

It requires action, especially in moments that feel uncomfortable, uncertain, or high-stakes. Justice-centered education calls for educators and leaders who are willing to examine how their own belief systems influence the way they think, communicate, and show up for students every day.

In this session, Coach Shaquan introduces the Check Your BS® (Belief System) transformational coaching framework alongside principles from Energy Leadership™ to explore how habitual thoughts shape behavior in educational spaces. These belief systems are often formed through what we have been taught, experienced, or assumed, and they influence how educators respond to students, engage in conversations around equity, and navigate moments that require clarity, courage, and accountability.

Equity will be defined as the ability to recognize how our beliefs, assumptions, and experiences influence how we treat others, and to make intentional choices that create fair and inclusive opportunities for every student to succeed. Participants will examine how “comfort” can show up as avoidance, silence, over-accommodation, or hesitation to address inequities in real time. The session challenges the belief that awareness alone leads to change by focusing on the internal patterns that create a gap between intention and impact.

This interactive experience introduces a practical, repeatable process to:

• Recognize whether current thoughts are supporting or limiting equitable practices
• Identify the source of beliefs and how they influence interactions with students and colleagues
• Name internal barriers such as fear of judgment, conflict, or getting it wrong
• Challenge beliefs that may no longer serve their role as educators
• Choose how to show up in alignment with equity, inclusion, and student success

By connecting self-awareness to everyday choice-making, this session supports educators in creating learning environments where students feel seen, supported, and valued. Participants will leave with language, tools, and a deeper understanding of how internal belief systems influence external outcomes.

This experience moves beyond inspiration and into transformation, equipping educators with tools they can immediately apply in their personal and professional relationships, as well as in their classrooms and leadership roles.

sessionTitle: From Compliance to Culture: Building Justice-Centered Systems that Strengthen Schools and Protect Student Futures
shortDescription:
Here’s a concise, SLU-ready version that keeps the core message intact:

Schools are navigating growing demands around compliance, student behavior, and equitable outcomes, yet many systems were built for accountability rather than belonging or development. This session introduces a Justice-Centered Systems Framework grounded in education reform, juvenile justice, restorative practices, and organizational leadership. It is a field-tested model that redefines how schools approach discipline, culture, and leadership alignment. Participants will examine why reactive discipline systems fall short and how to build proactive environments where belonging is embedded into the structure. Through real-world examples, attendees will leave with practical strategies and a clear, actionable path forward.
extendedDescription:
Schools across the country are navigating an increasingly complex intersection of compliance demands, student behavioral challenges, instructional expectations, and the urgent call for equitable outcomes. Yet many of the systems guiding school operations were designed primarily for accountability and risk management, not for fostering belonging, development, or long-term student success. This session directly aligns with the theme Justice-Centered Education for a Changing World by challenging participants to rethink how systems either reinforce inequities or intentionally disrupt them.

This session introduces a Justice-Centered Systems Framework grounded in the intersection of education reform, juvenile justice, restorative practices, and organizational leadership. The approach moves beyond theory and centers on a field-tested model that redefines how schools and districts address discipline, compliance, culture, and leadership alignment. Participants will explore how traditional, reactive discipline pipelines disproportionately impact students and staff, and how to shift toward proactive, system-level strategies that embed equity and belonging into daily practice.

The session will be structured in three parts. First, participants will engage in a systems reflection exercise to assess current practices and identify gaps in alignment, equity, and student experience. Second, the facilitator will introduce the Justice-Centered Systems Framework, highlighting key components such as restorative practices, leadership clarity, and sustainable culture-building strategies. Finally, participants will work through real-world case examples from charter schools, public districts, alternative education settings, and juvenile justice environments to translate concepts into actionable strategies.

By the end of the session, participants will leave with practical tools, implementation strategies, and a clear roadmap for creating justice-centered systems that improve student outcomes, strengthen school culture, and support sustainable change in an evolving educational landscape.

sessionTitle: Improving Teacher Support of Students with Anxiety in Catholic Elementary Schools
shortDescription: As the United States faces a mental health crisis, 3.1% of Americans aged 3-17 are affected by anxiety.  Through their vocation, Catholic school teachers are called to serve these students as children of God.  This session reviews concepts of improvement science as they relate to better understanding current systems as well as using data to begin positive change.
extendedDescription:
Introduction
This presentation will be created on Mentimeter.  Those in the session will be able to access the presentation through a QR code and participate in various features.
This presentation will begin by introducing the state of mental health in the United States.  It will provide statistics on mental health and anxiety in children as well as describe characteristics of generalized anxiety.
Topic 1: Anxiety in Schools
This section will address how anxiety affects students in elementary school.  Hamm’s video Understanding Trauma: Learning Brain vs Survival Brain will be used to describe how anxiety affects students.  Participants will be asked to participate in Mentimeter’s Select Answer feature to respond to the prompt, “Have you had students that seem to have survival brain in the classroom?”
The presentation will then address how Catholic school teachers are positioned to serve students with anxiety through a vocation to participate in the Church’s evangelizing mission (Miller, 2006), leading by Christ’s example.
Topic 2: Social Emotional Learning
This section of the presentation will focus on social emotional learning (SEL).  Its definition and how it contributes to mental health in schools will be discussed, citing the Collaborative for Academic, Social, and Emotional Learning and its graphics.
Openlight Media’s Catholic SEL framework will be presented, and Villa & Rassmussen’s parallel between the Carthusian motto “The cross of Christ is still in the turning world” and teacher vocation will be discussed.
Results from Hamilton & Doss’ 2019 teacher survey on SEL will be presented.  Then, participants will answer some of the same survey questions using Mentimeter’s Scales feature.  For example, participants will respond to questions about self-efficacy and responsibility regarding SEL on a scale.
Topic 3: Improvement Science
This section of the presentation will introduce Bryk et, al.’s definitions and concepts of improvement science and how teachers can use them to improve practice when it comes to meeting the needs of students with anxiety in the classroom.
The presentation will describe how a casual systems analysis is one method that can be used by teachers through improvement networks to identify what needs to be improved.  Journey lines and focus groups will be defined and examples will be provided.  Participants will have time to brainstorm responses to the question, “Reflect on one or two experiences within the last seven years in which you adjusted your routines and/or instruction to meet the needs of a student with characteristics of generalized anxiety.  Be sure to note any obstacles you encountered during these experiences.”  Then, participants will be able to share answers using Mentimeter’s Open Ended feature, which will then contribute to a focus group discussion.
Saldaña’s qualitative coding and fishbone diagrams will be discussed.  Together, the group will create a fishbone diagram and discuss next steps: networked improvement communities, an improvement map, and a driver diagram.
Conclusion
The presentation will end with an overview of topics addressed as well as a question and answer session.  Mentimenter’s Q&A feature will be used to present the questions to everyone for facilitated discussion.
takeaways: Better understand the United States Mental Health Crisis as it relates to anxiety and its effects on elementary school students
Use improvement science to begin identifying obstacles in meeting the needs of students with anxiety in your space
Collaborate with colleagues to create an improvement community focused on a mutual goal of student support
Connect more deeply with your vocation in becoming an agent of change

sessionTitle: Empathy & Restoring Harm: You Can't Have One without the Other
shortDescription:
Are you looking for guidance in addressing the social-emotional needs of students and staff?
This training connects the core principles from the book Circle Forward: Building a Restorative School Community to the practice of empathy and repairing harm. By gaining a thorough understanding of empathy, restorative practices, and the distinctions between dialogue and discussion, school leaders will enhance their essential skills of unconditional positive regard and presumption of positive intentions.
extendedDescription:
Our primary message to the educators in our session will be this:
In our school community, despite our best efforts at unconditional positive regard, we still struggle. The best way to conquer this hurdle is to hone your empathy skills and build your restorative toolkit. You will leave with artifacts and information so you can foster a welcoming school community no matter the situation.

During our session, we will lead participants through the following learning opportunities:
1. Awareness of the 8 tenets of empathy
2. Awareness of the foundational principles of restorative practices
3. How a formal circle works and feels
4. Restorative re-entry plan for students

We will place an emphasis on how, in schools, recognizing our own social identity, building empathy, participating in formal circles, and embracing restorative practices creates a brave, "equitable, inclusive, and impactful learning environment." 

The session will involve direct instruction, self-reflection by the participants in identifying their own social identities, and immersion into a circle. A culminating activity will be a "dip" into the waters of restorative practices by sharing a restorative re-entry plan for students who have been suspended, incarcerated, or absent from school for a long time.

sessionTitle: Equity Through Efficacy: How Collective Beliefs Transform Student Outcomes
shortDescription: Discover how Collective Teacher Efficacy (CTE) can serve as a powerful lever for equity in today’s schools. This session shares key findings from a district-level study, highlighting how leadership, trust, and collaboration shape collective beliefs and student outcomes. Participants will explore practical strategies to strengthen CTE, break down systemic barriers, and create more equitable, inclusive learning environments where all students can thrive.
extendedDescription:
In today’s schools, we have seen more and more isolated "silos" of teachers and grade levels in each school. This continues to remind us that Collective Efficacy must be built into how educators think, collaborate, and take responsibility for student success. Collective Teacher Efficacy is one of the most powerful drivers of student achievement, yet many schools struggle to intentionally develop it. This session centers on a simple but critical idea: when educators truly believe in their collective ability to impact all students, their actions—and outcomes—change.

Drawing from a district-level mixed-methods study, this session explores how leadership, trust, and collaboration directly influence the development of CTE. Grounded in Bandura’s Social Cognitive Theory, this research highlights a key tension in schools today: while we ask teachers to produce equitable outcomes, we often fail to create the conditions that allow collective belief and shared responsibility to thrive .

This work is deeply connected to justice-centered education. Equity is about ensuring every student experiences high expectations, strong instruction, and a team of educators who refuse to let them fail. It is so much more than mere access. CTE shifts the focus from individual classrooms to collective ownership. When schools operate from a place of “these are our students,” rather than “my students vs. your students,” systems begin to change. Barriers are challenged. Instruction improves. Outcomes follow.

Participants will examine key findings around leadership practices that strengthen or weaken collective efficacy, reflect on the current state of trust and collaboration within their own systems, and identify common barriers such as time, fragmentation, and inconsistent expectations. The session will also provide practical, actionable strategies, including strengthening PLC structures, building trust through intentional leadership moves, and using data as a tool for collective problem-solving rather than compliance.

This session is designed for educators and leaders who are ready to move beyond surface-level collaboration and into meaningful, equity-driven work. Justice-centered education is not about what one teacher can do. but more about what we are willing to believe and accomplish together.

sessionTitle: Stop Burning Out: The Unfreeze Formula for ADHD Educators
shortDescription: Burnout isn't just overworking. It's self-abandonment in every area of your life. You freeze in meetings. You have 17 brilliant ideas and start none of them. You get asked a direct question and your brain goes blank. This workshop delivers specific tools for specific freeze moments, so you can keep overwhelm from turning into full-blown burnout. Plus, one question that changes everything. Walk away grounded, clear, and able to start when you're stopped.
extendedDescription:
You have seventeen ideas that would change everything. You've started none of them, because you don't know where to start.

You're in a meeting. Someone says something that's not right. You feel it in your chest. You say nothing. You drive home replaying it for two hours.

Someone asks you a direct question. Your prefrontal cortex takes a vacation. Your mouth opens. Nothing comes out.

These aren't character flaws that need fixed. It's a nervous system doing exactly what it's designed to do, experiencing the mismatch between how you're wired and how we're taught leadership. You're an ADHD educator - brilliant, nonlinear, pattern-recognizing, carrying everyone's feelings and expectations, susceptible to overwhelm and burnout in the process.

Burnout isn't just overworking. It's self-abandonment in every area of your life. And self-abandonment starts with the freeze.

This 90-minute interactive workshop teaches you three specific tools for three specific freeze moments.
First, you'll learn and practice a somatic reset for that in-the-moment freeze. Because you cannot think your way out of a dysregulated nervous system. This is a body-based practice you can utilize literally anywhere, in any moment when your mind and body simply seize up

Second, we'll dive into the Unfreeze Formula. You've found ground again with the somatic reset, now here are the five steps to start wen you're stopped. We'll focus on the ones that matter most for educators: finding possibility ("it's possible this isn't a dumb idea"), checking your needs (hungry? tired? antsy?), and identifying the single lowest-barrier next step. The goal isn't to finish everything. It's to start what matters most.

Lastly, master the 10-minute ask. As an educator often put on the spot, you can stop beating yourself up when your brain goes blank. The 10-minute ask is learning to advocate for yourself, and putting it into regular practice is how you model advocating for yourself to others watching - your own leaders, your colleagues, and your students.

Throughout the workshop, we'll return to one question: What am I holding that was never mine to carry? The extra email at 10pm. The colleague's emotional reaction. The committee work that should have been shared. The feeling that you have to do all the things that need done.

When you stop carrying what isn't yours, you have energy for what is. And when you advocate for yourself - when you unfreeze, when you ask for ten minutes, when you take the somatic reset before speaking - you model that for every person in your classroom, your building, your district. That's justice-centered education. Not just for the students. For you.

Come ready to move, to name what freezes you, and to leave with tools you can use tomorrow.
takeaways: After this session
participants will be able to:
~Use a 60-second somatic reset before any high-stakes conversation
~Apply the 5-step Unfreeze Formula to start any task they've been avoiding
~Advocate for themselves in the moment.
~Identify one thing they're carrying that was never theirs — and practice setting it down

sessionTitle: Why is St. Louis So Segregated? How Past Housing Discrimination Shaped Districts
shortDescription: There are 23 geographic school districts in St. Louis City and County, and district boundary lines can determine which students have access to educational opportunity in our region. Per pupil spending ranges from $11,585/year in Bayless to $23,500/year in Clayton, while percent White ranges from 0.5% to 82.4%. How did this come to be? This presentation examines the history of housing discrimination in St. Louis and how it directly impacts students in schools today.
extendedDescription: This presentation is based upon an undergraduate course I developed and teach called "Unveiling the Color Line: Investigating the Formation and Segregation of St. Louis School Districts.” The presentation will start with an investigation of current disparities in table groups. I will share the excellent video Still Separate, Still Unequal (Forward Through Ferguson) and help participants to understand the relationship between school funding, property taxes, and housing discrimination. I will share a brief lecture on the history of racial housing discrimination, including racial zoning, real estate practices, restrictive covenants, redlining, the federal support of White-only suburbs (and the resulting White flight), and how municipal zoning in the county was used to segregate by income and race. Participants will examine interactive maps from the Mapping Decline (Web Project) and St. Louis Public Radio. I will also share a clip from Richard Rothstein’s excellent video Segregated By Design. We will talk briefly about school desegregation and how the current boundary lines were drawn. We will finish our time together discussing opportunities students have had to “cross the color line” through VICC and the Student Transfer Law and consider other policy solutions that have been proposed.
takeaways: Describe how past racial housing discrimination shaped school district segregation.
Describe the limits of past attempts to address district segregation in our region.
Explain pros/cons of possible policy solutions.
focusAreas: Historical causes of inequality across school districts

sessionTitle: Dangerous Learning: Literacy, Truth, and Liberation through Curriculum
shortDescription: Grounded in Dangerous Learning by Derek W. Black, this session examines how the historic suppression of Black literacy connects to present-day book bans, curriculum censorship, and the fight for truthful teaching. Participants will explore literacy as liberation, engage culturally responsive strategies aligned with the Science of Reading, and leave with practical tools to center Black voices, resist erasure, and reclaim curriculum as a space of empowerment.
extendedDescription:
Dangerous Learning: Literacy, Truth, and Liberation through Curriculum is a justice-centered session I developed that examines how the historical suppression of Black literacy continues to echo in today’s battles over book bans, curriculum censorship, and access to truthful teaching. Grounded in Derek W. Black’s Dangerous Learning, this session invites educators to consider literacy not simply as a technical skill, but as a site of power, resistance, and human dignity.

The session connects directly to the theme “Justice-Centered Education for a Changing World” by challenging participants to confront how inequity is often reproduced through what schools choose to teach, omit, sanitize, or silence. In a time when many educators are navigating political pressure, shifting standards, and growing demands for culturally responsive instruction, this presentation offers both historical grounding and practical direction. It asks a central question: How can educators create learning spaces that tell the truth, affirm identity, and equip students to read both the word and the world?

I designed the session as a Critical Dialogue, blending presenter-guided content with participant reflection and collaboration. The session opens with a brief historical arc tracing Black literacy resistance through the Stono Rebellion, David Walker’s Appeal, Nat Turner, and postbellum literacy bans. From there, participants examine present-day examples of curriculum restriction and discuss how schools either reinforce or resist erasure.

A major feature of the session is the emphasis on solutions. Participants will engage three practical strategies aligned with the Science of Reading and culturally responsive pedagogy: oral storytelling routines for oral language development, decoding practice through culturally relevant texts, and liberation literacy mini-lessons that support comprehension, identity, and critical thinking. I believe these strategies are designed to help educators move beyond abstract commitments to equity and toward concrete classroom practice.

Participants will also engage in dialogue around curriculum reclamation, asking how we shift from teaching about Black people to teaching with, for, and through Black experiences. The session includes collaborative time for attendees to begin designing tools, lesson ideas, or literacy practices they can immediately apply in classrooms, schools, community settings, and family engagement spaces.

By the end of the session, educators will leave with a stronger historical understanding of literacy justice, a clearer framework for justice-centered instruction, and practical resources that support inclusive and impactful learning environments. The session will be more than a presentation about censorship or resistance alone,  it’s a call to teach with courage, depth, and purpose in a changing world.

This interactive session explores how trauma-informed, culturally responsive wellness practices can support justice-centered education by helping educators create learning environments where youth feel safe, affirmed, regulated, and connected. Grounded in The Oxygen Project: Yoga for Youth’s community-based model, the session positions wellness as an essential part of educational equity, particularly for students navigating the impacts of systemic racism, community violence, chronic stress, trauma, and disinvestment.
The session connects directly to the theme, “Justice-Centered Education for a Changing World,” by challenging educators to think beyond traditional academic support and consider the emotional, social, cultural, and embodied needs of students. Justice-centered education requires classrooms and school communities that do not rely solely on punitive or deficit-based responses to student behavior, but instead recognize behavior, disconnection, anxiety, and stress as often rooted in larger systemic conditions. Through this lens, yoga, breathwork, mindfulness, and social-emotional learning become tools for healing, belonging, and student empowerment.
The session will begin with a brief presentation on The Oxygen Project’s work in schools and community-based youth spaces across the St. Louis region. This portion will highlight how culturally responsive wellness programming can support emotional regulation, self-awareness, youth voice, and a stronger sense of belonging. Participants will be invited to reflect on how race, trauma, school climate, and student well-being intersect, and how educators can integrate healing-centered approaches into their daily practice.
The second portion of the session will be experiential. Participants will engage in accessible breathing, grounding, mindfulness, and gentle movement practices that can be adapted for classrooms, advisory periods, staff meetings, youth programs, or personal self-care. These practices will be designed to support both students and the adults who serve them, recognizing that educators also need tools for regulation, restoration, and collective care.
The session will close with reflection and application. Participants will identify practical ways to incorporate healing-centered practices into their own learning environments and consider how these strategies can contribute to more equitable, inclusive, and impactful educational spaces. Attendees will leave with a deeper understanding of wellness as a justice issue and with concrete tools to support student belonging, emotional well-being, and transformative learning.

sessionTitle: Move, Saboteur, Get Out Of the Way: Confronting the Self-Saboteurs that Prevent You from Self-Actualization
shortDescription:
As Dr. Zaretta Hammond discusses in Culturally Responsive Teaching and the Brain, the role of “lizard brain” is to protect us from “threats” that might cause hurt, harm, danger, risk, embarrassment, “exposure,” etc. The danger or threat that the brain senses could be either physical, psychological, or socio-emotional. You may recognize Imposter Syndrome and Stereotype Threat as manifestations of the brain-body reaction to psychological/socio-emotional threats. 

There are at least nine other ways you react to stressors. These stressors, or saboteurs, if not addressed, can prevent you from reaching your full potential.

In this workshop, you will explore how you might unconsciously be your own “worst enemy” - your self-saboteur. Walking away from this session, you will use a “saboteur assessment” to learn your top 9 limiting behaviors and mindsets. You will also commit to 3-5 strategies to help you confront and neutralize the specific ways you self-sabotage.
extendedDescription:
“Move, Sab(oteur), Get Out the Way: Confronting Your Self-Saboteurs” is an interactive, neuroscience-informed leadership and equity session designed to help educators, school leaders, and organizational teams better understand how stress, identity, trauma, fear, and self-protective behaviors shape the way we lead, teach, communicate, and build relationships. Rooted in culturally responsive pedagogy, emotional intelligence, leadership coaching, and educational neuroscience, the session challenges participants to examine the internal barriers that often prevent transformative leadership and equitable practice.

The session begins by establishing community, vulnerability, and trust. Participants engage in reflective storytelling, pair-share protocols, and culturally resonant multimedia experiences that encourage them to examine moments of stress, frustration, imposter syndrome, fear, exhaustion, and emotional dissonance in both their personal and professional lives. Drawing heavily from the work of Dr. Zaretta Hammond and Dr. James Comer, the session explores how the brain responds to perceived social and psychological threats and how those responses influence learning, leadership, relationships, and organizational culture.

Participants are then introduced to the neuroscience of the “lizard brain,” David Rock’s SCARF framework, and the concept of self-saboteurs as protective yet limiting behavioral patterns. The session examines nine common saboteur profiles, including Hyper-Achiever, Pleaser, Controller, Hyper-Vigilant, Stickler, and Avoider. Rather than framing these tendencies as flaws, the workshop helps participants understand how strengths, survival mechanisms, and cultural conditioning can become overextended in high-stress environments.

A central feature of the session is helping educators identify the difference between their “royal self” — the aspects of their identity and leadership that create resonance, purpose, and impact — and their “wolf self,” the reactive tendencies that emerge under stress. Through guided reflection activities, discussion protocols, assessment tools, and emotional intelligence exercises, participants identify the internal narratives, fears, and triggers that may negatively influence communication, feedback, collaboration, conflict management, decision-making, and instructional leadership.

The session directly connects to the conference theme, “Justice-Centered Education for a Changing World,” because justice-centered work requires more than technical knowledge or instructional strategies; it requires deep self-awareness, emotional regulation, relational trust, and courageous leadership. Educators cannot sustainably create equitable and inclusive environments while unconsciously operating from fear, avoidance, perfectionism, defensiveness, or burnout. This workshop helps participants recognize how internalized stress responses and identity-based pressures can unintentionally reproduce inequitable systems, harmful interactions, or exclusionary leadership practices.

Ultimately, the session equips educators with practical strategies to “domesticate the wolf” by building emotional intelligence, psychological safety, authentic relationships, and reflective leadership habits. Participants leave with concrete tools, individualized reflection protocols, and actionable strategies to confront self-limiting behaviors, strengthen relational leadership, and cultivate learning environments where students and adults feel psychologically safe, seen, valued, and empowered to thrive.

sessionTitle: From Access to Belonging: Designing Systems for All Learners
shortDescription: How can special education systems move beyond compliance to create meaningful access and belonging? This interactive session examines how special education can more effectively support access to the general curriculum through strong Tier 1 instruction, thoughtful use of specially designed instruction, and collaborative systems. Participants will explore common barriers and leave with practical strategies to improve outcomes for students with disabilities.
extendedDescription:
This session examines how special education systems can move beyond compliance-driven practices to more effectively support access, belonging, and meaningful outcomes for students with disabilities. Grounded in current research and practical experience, the session reframes special education as part of a broader, integrated system in which general education, specially designed instruction, and schoolwide structures work together to ensure equitable access to the general curriculum.

The session is organized around three key shifts. First, participants will explore the shift from viewing special education as a separate system to recognizing the central role of high-quality Tier 1 instruction in driving outcomes for students with disabilities. Second, the session will clarify the purpose of specially designed instruction, emphasizing how adapting content, methodology, and delivery supports access rather than replacing grade-level expectations. Third, participants will examine the distinction between inclusion and belonging, considering how school environments can move beyond physical placement to ensure students feel accepted, supported, and engaged.

This interactive session is designed for a broad audience, including general educators, special educators, school leaders, and higher education faculty. Participants will engage in structured discussions, analyze classroom and systems-level scenarios, and apply learning through guided activities. These activities include identifying common system breakdowns that limit access, redesigning instructional approaches using principles of specially designed instruction, and reflecting on how school structures such as scheduling, collaboration, and professional learning influence outcomes for students with disabilities.

This session directly aligns with the theme, Justice-Centered Education for a Changing World, by addressing how inequities are often embedded within well-intended systems. When special education is treated as a separate solution rather than a shared responsibility, students with disabilities may experience reduced access to grade-level instruction, lower expectations, and limited opportunities for meaningful participation. By focusing on access, high expectations, and belonging, this session supports educators in identifying and disrupting these patterns.

Participants will leave with practical strategies and a clear framework to guide their work, including tools for evaluating access to the general curriculum, strengthening the use of specially designed instruction, and fostering inclusive environments that promote both academic and social success.

extendedDescription:
This session explores how educators can intentionally design mathematics instruction that affirms and empowers emergent bilingual students, positioning their linguistic and cultural assets as strengths rather than barriers. Grounded in the conference theme, “Justice-Centered Education for a Changing World,” the session emphasizes that equitable math instruction is not only about access to content, but also about disrupting deficit narratives, expanding participation, and ensuring all students can engage meaningfully in mathematical thinking.

Participants will examine how traditional approaches to math instruction often privilege language-heavy, monolingual norms that can marginalize emergent bilingual learners. Through a justice-centered lens, we will reframe these practices by highlighting the importance of multiple representations, discourse-rich classrooms, and culturally sustaining pedagogies.

The session is structured in three parts. First, participants will engage in a brief interactive task that simulates the experience of navigating math learning in an unfamiliar language. This activity builds empathy and surfaces common barriers students may face. Next, we will explore high-impact instructional strategies, including the use of visuals and manipulatives, structured math discourse routines, sentence frames, and opportunities for collaborative problem-solving. Real classroom examples and student work will be shared to illustrate how these strategies can be implemented across grade levels.

In the final portion, participants will apply their learning by analyzing and adapting a math task to better support emergent bilinguals. Working in small groups, educators will identify potential linguistic and cultural barriers and redesign the task using the strategies discussed. This collaborative planning time ensures that participants leave with practical, ready-to-use ideas.

Attendees will leave with concrete strategies, a deeper understanding of equitable math instruction, and a renewed commitment to creating inclusive environments where all students’ identities and abilities are valued.

sessionTitle: Building Instructional Coherence: A Justice-Centered Approach to Teacher Practice, Engagement, and Coaching
shortDescription: Building Instructional Coherence: A Justice-Centered Approach to Teaching and Learning explores how aligned systems of coaching, PLCs, and data use can transform teacher practice and student engagement. Grounded in a four-year design-based model, this session highlights practical tools and strategies to ensure all students experience rigorous, inclusive instruction. Participants will leave with actionable approaches to create coherent, equitable learning environments that support meaningful outcomes for every learner.
extendedDescription:
This session, “Building Instructional Coherence: A Justice-Centered Approach to Teacher Practice, Engagement, and Coaching,” presents a multi-year, design-based framework for strengthening instructional consistency, teacher effectiveness, and student engagement through aligned systems of support. Grounded in translational research, the session bridges the gap between theory and classroom practice by demonstrating how research-informed strategies can be adapted, tested, and sustained in real-world school contexts.
At its core, the session advances three key ideas. First, instructional coherence—the alignment of curriculum, pedagogy, assessment, and professional learning—is essential for equitable student outcomes. Fragmented systems disproportionately impact historically underserved students; thus, coherence becomes a justice issue. Second, teacher practice is the primary lever for change. Through structured coaching cycles, observation rubrics, and actionable feedback, educators refine high-impact strategies such as modeling, scaffolding, and purposeful questioning. Third, student engagement and self-regulation are central outcomes, with particular attention to increasing the number of students in productive learning states and ensuring that all students have access to rigorous, meaningful instruction.
The approach is anchored in a four-year design-based research model that emphasizes iterative improvement. Year 1 focuses on identifying challenges such as low engagement and inconsistent instructional practices. Years 2 and 3 introduce targeted interventions, including data-driven Professional Learning Communities (PLCs), coaching tools, and instructional frameworks. By Year 4, the model aims to achieve sustained coherence, improved teacher practice, and higher levels of student engagement. This iterative cycle allows educators to continuously test, refine, and scale practices based on evidence, ensuring responsiveness to diverse student needs.

Structurally, the session is organized into three segments:

Problem Framing and Equity Lens – Participants examine how inequities manifest through inconsistent instruction and limited data use.
Model and Tools – Introduction to the instructional framework, coaching rubrics, and PLC protocols that support aligned practice and actionable data use.
Application and Reflection – Educators analyze sample data, set instructional goals, and plan implementation within their own contexts.

This session directly aligns with the theme “Justice-Centered Education for a Changing World” by positioning coherence and consistency as mechanisms for equity. When all students experience high-quality, research-based instruction—regardless of classroom or teacher—schools move closer to justice-centered outcomes. The model also prioritizes inclusive practices by supporting teachers in differentiating instruction, fostering student voice, and building self-regulation skills.
Ultimately, the session equips educators with practical tools, collaborative structures, and a research-backed framework to create equitable, inclusive, and impactful learning environments. By integrating coaching, PLCs, and data-informed decision-making, participants leave with a clear, actionable pathway to transform instructional systems in ways that are sustainable and justice-driven.

sessionTitle: Research to Action: Effective Reading Strategies in Every Classroom
shortDescription: What does the science on reading actually say and what does it mean for teachers on Monday morning? This session grounds educators in the foundational research behind skilled reading, from the Matthew Effect to Scarborough's Reading Rope, and connects it directly to classroom practice. Participants leave with a clear, actionable framework for understanding how students become readers and how instruction must meet them where they are.
extendedDescription:
Reading is not a natural act. It must be deliberately taught and the research tells us exactly how. Yet too often, teachers are handed curricula or mandates without the deeper "why" that makes instruction stick. This session bridges that gap.

Grounded in three cornerstone research studies (the Matthew Effect, the ACT Study, and the Baseball Study), participants explore the science that undergirds effective literacy instruction. First, The Matthew Effect reveals how early reading gaps compound over time, making the quality of foundational instruction a matter of equity, not just achievement. Second, The ACT Study reframes the role of text complexity and knowledge building, challenging the assumption that reading strategy instruction is the primary lever for growth. Third, The Baseball Study delivers perhaps the most clarifying insight: prior knowledge, not decoding skill alone, drives comprehension, upending how many educators have been taught to teach reading.

From there, the session contextualizes Scarborough's Reading Rope as a unifying framework, helping participants understand how word recognition and language comprehension develop interdependently and why comprehension is an outcome, not a standalone skill to be practiced in isolation. This reframe has direct implications for how teachers structure time, select texts, and sequence instruction across a lesson and a school year.

The session then traces the connection from Scarborough's model to the Missouri Learning Standards, situating research within the curriculum and assessment contexts participants already navigate. Teachers practice identifying alignment between the Sound Spelling Acquisition Continuum (Hear It, Say It, Read It, Write It) and their own instructional materials, leaving with something they can use immediately.

This session connects directly to the conference theme of Justice-Centered Education for a Changing World by naming the truth plainly: the reading gap is an equity gap. Students who don't become readers in the early grades face compounding disadvantages across every subject, every year. Closing that gap demands that educators at every level understand not just what to teach, but why the science points there and how instruction must evolve to serve every learner.

Articulate why comprehension is an outcome of strong word recognition and language comprehension
not a standalone skill to be practiced in isolation

sessionTitle: Belonging by Design: Building Just and Sustainable School Systems
shortDescription: Many schools attempt to improve outcomes for diverse learners while operating within fragmented systems where interventions, classroom instruction, support services, and educator development are disconnected. This session explores how schools can move from isolated supports to integrated, inclusive systems strengthening instruction, student belonging, and long-term sustainability. Participants will examine how school systems shape adult practice, what new educators learn from the environments they enter, and how intentional collaboration and coaching structures can sustain inclusive practices.
extendedDescription:
Belonging by Design: Building Just and Sustainable School Systems explores if we are asking the right questions about how schools can create equitable and inclusive learning environments. Are we strengthening the systems that shape instructional practice, collaboration, and student experience? While many schools are committed to improving outcomes for diverse learners, interventions, classroom instruction, student support services, and educator development often operate in disconnected ways. These fragmented systems can unintentionally weaken instructional access, reduce consistency for students, and make inclusive practices difficult to sustain over time.
Grounded in the theme “Justice-Centered Education for a Changing World,” this session questions the role school systems play in shaping both student belonging and adult practice. Justice-centered education requires intentional structures that support equitable access, collaborative problem-solving, and shared responsibility for student success, it is not an individual responsibility. Participants will explore how school cultures are reproduced through daily systems, routines, adult interactions, and instructional norms, and how new educators often adopt the practices modeled within the environments they enter, regardless of their pre-service preparation or personal mission.
This interactive session is designed for system designers, instructional leaders, and interventionists, seeking practical strategies to strengthen inclusive practice through embedding sustainable systems. Participants will engage in guided activities that connect systems-level reflection to actionable implementation.
The session will begin with an “Intervention Disconnect Audit,” where participants analyze common areas of fragmentation between instruction, interventions, and support systems within schools. Through structured reflection and discussion, participants will identify how disconnected systems can unintentionally create barriers for students and contribute to educator inconsistency and burnout. Together, we will engage in a “Student Experience Mapping” activity focused on belonging, identity, and instructional access. Using real-world scenarios, participants will examine how students experience school systems throughout the day and identify moments where systems either strengthen or undermine inclusion and belonging. We are committed to providing activities that center the lived experiences of students while helping educators recognize how instructional and organizational decisions impact equity in practice.
In our time, we will also explore the importance of adult modeling, coaching, and collaborative structures in sustaining inclusion practices. Participants will reflect on the messages new educators receive from school systems about collaboration, intervention ownership, inclusion, and instructional expectations. Emphasis will be placed on creating embedded systems that move beyond isolated efforts and instead support long-term sustainability through coaching, shared ownership, and aligned adult practices.
Participants will leave with practical tools and strategies to examine their own systems, and develop one actionable next step for guiding their schools to strengthen collaboration across teams.

sessionTitle: University City's Rising Tide: How We Got Every Classroom Rowing Together
shortDescription: Change is hard. Scaling change is harder. Learn how University City Schools partnered with Updraft Education to align an entire district around a shared vision of great instruction and got 98% of teachers on board in a year. We'll walk through the systems, data, and change management moves that made it work, and how you can adapt them to your context.
extendedDescription:
Most school systems are a complex blend of humans, systems, legacy programs, inertia, and new ideas and priorities. Despite incredible effort, meaningful change can feel daunting and sometimes impossible. The cost of that complexity falls hardest on the students who most need school to work for them. When practices vary widely from classroom to classroom, the quality of a child's day depends on which teacher they were assigned, which schedule they happened to receive, which adult was watching when something went wrong. Consistency is one of the most direct expressions of justice a school can offer.


This session tells the story of how University City School District, in partnership with Updraft Education, built that consistency in a single year. A year ago, teachers across the district were navigating dozens of tools and competing perspectives, each well-intentioned, none fully aligned. Today, educators across University City Schools demonstrate strong belief and commitment to the Learning Core Commitments, with 98% reporting they are committed to establishing the commitments in their classrooms and 91% believing the work can create consistently high-quality experiences for all students. Walkthrough data supports this, with 95% of observed indicators marked as present during classroom visits.


Dr. Sharonica Hardin and Dr. Elizabeth Gardner of University City will share what the work looked like from the inside. They will speak honestly about what changed, what was hard, and what required them to lead differently. Brad White of Updraft Education will walk through the Rise Together framework that anchored the partnership: how to identify the right shared commitments, how to build a guiding coalition that carries the work forward across roles and buildings, and how to sustain alignment past the initial energy of a launch.


The connection to this year's theme is direct. Justice-centered education shows up in whether every student can count on the same caliber of instruction, the same culture of belonging, and the same expectations no matter which door they walk through. When schools align instruction and culture, the students who have historically been served least well by inconsistent systems are the ones whose experience improves most. This work is structural and it is moral, and it is one of the highest-leverage commitments a system can make to the children it serves.
Participants will leave with a clear understanding of the conditions that made the University City partnership succeed, a practical view of the Rise Together framework and the role of a guiding coalition, and a draft starting point for applying this pathway to a priority or change area in their own school or system. We will close with time for questions, peer exchange, and one-on-one conversation with the presenters.

sessionTitle: Words That Matter: Using Gender-Affirming Language to Support LGBTQ+ Students
shortDescription: Inclusive language matters, but putting it into practice can feel intimidating. This session helps educators overcome challenges in using gender-affirming language by exploring practical, implementable strategies. With the expert guidance of a nonbinary former English teacher, you'll increase your confidence with affirming language and create a more supportive school community. This is a judgment-free space where mistakes and tough questions are welcome, with opportunities for honest dialogue about supporting LGBTQ+ students in today’s sociopolitical climate.
extendedDescription:
According to a national student survey, 68% of LGBTQ+ youth report feeling unsafe at school. Too many students show up each day to environments where they don’t feel welcome: where peers bully and tease, there’s no safe bathroom to use, and people like them are erased from the curriculum and community. This statistic is more than a number; it is a call to action.

In my 15+ years supporting LGBTQ+ students in K-12 and higher education, I have identified a range of evidence-based strategies that help shift schools toward cultures of belonging. One of the most powerful and accessible is the consistent use of gender-affirming language across all forms of communication, from classroom instruction to hallway conversations to school-wide policies.

Research consistently shows that affirming language can reduce LGBTQ+ youth suicide risk by up to 35%, decrease rates of depression and anxiety, and improve self-esteem, belonging, and feelings of safety. Words are powerful, and this session equips educators to use that power intentionally.

In the current sociopolitical climate, many educators face barriers to implementing inclusive practices. Some must navigate policies shaped by anti-LGBTQ+ agendas rather than best practices. Others fear saying the “wrong thing” or causing harm, while many simply lack models of what affirming language looks like in practice.

The “Words That Matter” workshop addresses these challenges by providing educators with clarity, confidence, and practical tools. Participants will explore the foundations of gender-affirming language, engage in activities they can use with students and families, reflect on real-world scenarios, and discuss the barriers they are navigating within their own schools.

The session is intentionally designed to encourage imperfect efforts, compassionate accountability, and low-stakes practice. Participants receive immediate feedback, peer support, and concrete resources including scripts, templates, and classroom tools they can implement right away. At the same time, we hold space for the reality of institutional barriers and the complexity educators face in this work.

This session aligns directly with the theme of “Justice-Centered Education for a Changing World” because language is a critical tool for educational equity and social justice. When educators use gender-affirming language, they disrupt systems that marginalize LGBTQ+ students, center the dignity of students who are often rendered invisible, and prepare young people for a world where understanding gender diversity is increasingly essential.

At a time when anti-LGBTQ+ legislation and rhetoric are intensifying, educators have a critical role to play as advocates and culture-shifters. This session empowers them to do that work not through grand gestures, but through the daily practice of using words that affirm, validate, and protect the students who need it most.

Rather than operating as isolated initiatives, these systems are intentionally connected through a shared improvement framework that keeps school goals visible, actionable, and measurable. sessionTitle: Building Life Skills Through Journaling: Creative Reflection as a Tool for Education
shortDescription:
This interactive workshop introduces educators to the power of journaling as a tool for social-emotional learning, confidence-building, creativity, and life skills development in the classroom.

Rather than simply discussing journaling strategies, educators will actively participate in several guided activities pulled directly from the Journaling for Life Skills student curriculum. Through reflection, music, creative writing, and discussion, participants will experience firsthand how journaling can support emotional regulation, self-expression, communication, and student engagement.

This workshop also serves as an introduction to the full 12-Week Journaling for Life Skills Program available for schools, after-school programs, and youth organizations serving students in grades 5–12.
extendedDescription:
“Journaling for Life Skills: Creative Reflection as a Tool for Education” is an interactive workshop designed to help educators integrate journaling, creative expression, and social-emotional learning into their classrooms in ways that support inclusion and student well-being. 
This session is grounded in the belief that justice-centered education must create space for students to process their emotions, explore their identities, and build confidence in their own perspectives and lived experiences. In a rapidly changing world, students need more than academic instruction alone — they need tools for emotional regulation, self-expression, communication, and reflection when they leave the classroom. Journaling is a skill that supports mental and emotional wellness for students and educators alike. Journaling offers an accessible and low-pressure way to support building crucial skills while helping educators cultivate more human-centered classroom environments.
Rather than presenting journaling as simply a writing exercise, this workshop introduces it as a life skills practice that can be adapted across grade levels and subject areas. Participants will actively engage in several guided activities pulled directly from my 12-week “Journaling for Life Skills” youth program for students in grades 5–12.
The session will begin with a brief grounding and reflective writing exercise that models how educators can use low-stakes journaling to create emotionally safe learning spaces and support students entering their classwork with a clear mind. 
Participants will then experience “The Goal Sun” confidence-building activity, where they identify a personal or professional goal and write affirmations around why they are capable of achieving it. This activity demonstrates how journaling can help students strengthen self-esteem, develop growth mindsets, and recognize their own strengths — particularly for students whose voices and identities are often marginalized in traditional educational settings.
Participants will also engage in a rhythm and musical expression activity in which they rewrite the chorus of a familiar song to reflect their current emotions or experiences. This activity highlights the importance of culturally responsive and creative approaches to learning while encouraging students to connect literacy, emotional awareness, and self-expression. Throughout the workshop, educators will discuss how journaling can support students navigating stress, identity development, overwhelm, and interpersonal challenges.
The workshop structure includes guided participation, individual reflection, small group discussion, and practical implementation strategies educators can bring back to their classrooms immediately. Participants will leave with concrete journaling prompts, activity ideas, and facilitation approaches that center emotional wellness, creativity, and student agency.
This session directly connects to the theme “Justice-Centered Education for a Changing World” by encouraging educators to rethink what student support can look like in today’s classrooms. Justice-centered education requires creating environments where students feel seen, heard, emotionally safe, and empowered to express themselves authentically. By incorporating reflective writing and creative journaling practices into the classroom, educators can build more inclusive learning environments that honor students’ humanity while strengthening the life skills they need to navigate an increasingly complex world.

Use culturally responsive and arts-based journaling prompts to increase student engagement and participation
Apply journaling techniques that help students build life skills such as communication
emotional regulation
and goal-setting.
Create more inclusive and emotionally safe learning environments that center student voice and lived experience

sessionTitle: Justice in Practice: How Schools and Leaders Navigate Discipline Challenges
shortDescription: This interactive workshop engages participants in examining justice‑centered approaches to school discipline. Drawing on statewide insights from Missouri, attendees will engage in reflective dialogue and collaborative design activities to explore disproportionality, gaps in current interventions, and the varied resource needs across districts. Participants will identify barriers, promising practices, and co‑develop strategies for creating equitable, inclusive, and safe learning environments for all students.
extendedDescription:
This 90‑minute interactive workshop will invite participants to focus on school climate and culture by transformative vision of school discipline systems in a rapidly changing world. Facilitated by Dr. Chhabra and Dr. Shahid, the session will blend brief research insights with active reflection, small‑group dialogue, and collaborative design activities.

While disproportionality in disciplinary consequences is well‑documented, far less is known about the current state of systems-level interventions and resources needed to address it. Grounded in findings from a statewide evaluation of disciplinary practices in Missouri, the workshop will engage participants to explore this structural gap, considering how school and district leaders perceive disciplinary challenges, what preventive or responsive approaches are currently being used, and where implementation barriers continue to limit progress. 

The facilitators will prompt participants to identify the systemic factors that shape disciplinary outcomes—translation of policies into practice, resource allocation, community context, sustainability of existing interventions, and availability of culturally responsive supports in schools. Participants will consider how rural, suburban, and urban districts experience different constraints and opportunities, and how these differences influence the feasibility and sustainability of justice‑centered interventions.

By the end of the workshop, attendees will leave with a clearer understanding of the current disciplinary landscape, a deeper awareness of the gaps in intervention practice, and a set of co‑created ideas for advancing justice‑centered discipline in their districts and schools.

sessionTitle: Partnering for Change: Strategies for Leaders to Partner for Growth
shortDescription: The UMSL Charter School Office will present on how leaders can build capacity for school-level translational research. While balancing support for continuous improvement with oversight boundaries, this partnership helped schools clarify and track social-emotional learning goals. Through cycles of inquiry, the sponsor and schools co-built systems to assess progress, determine initiative effectiveness, and adjust practices. This collaborative capacity-building empowers charter schools to drive authentic growth and communicate success beyond traditional academic measures.
extendedDescription:
The UMSL Charter School Office will present on how leaders can build capacity for school-level translational research, specifically centering the theme of "Justice-Centered Education for a Changing World." True educational justice requires moving beyond rigid, traditional academic compliance measures that historically disadvantage marginalized student populations to look holistically at student well-being. This session details how a unique partnership, leveraging leadership roles and community partners, helped schools clarify, measure, and make visible their social-emotional learning (SEL) goals, ensuring that equity and student care remain at the forefront of school design.

Navigating the space between a sponsor’s oversight boundaries and a school’s need for autonomy requires a careful balance. By leveraging a collaborative framework, this partnership utilized Rapid Cycles of Inquiry to co-build systems that assess progress, determine the effectiveness of initiatives, and adjust practices in real-time. A tangible outcome of this work, an SEL measurement and systems-tracking tool developed directly alongside the partnering schools, will be shared with attendees to demonstrate how abstract equity goals can be translated into practical school-level systems. Additionally, this can be a point where schools look beyond traditional measures of success, such as academic scores, and gain insight into what might be valuable in telling their story.

Ultimately, this presentation serves as a roadmap for how school leaders can become critical consumers of external partnerships. Leaders face immense pressure to drive rapid systems change but often lack the specialized capacity, time, or research infrastructure to build complex measurement frameworks internally. By strategically leveraging outside expertise, like a university research fellow, leaders can inject rigorous, justice-centered research methods into their buildings without losing their school's unique identity. This intentional collaboration empowers leaders to co-construct the internal systems necessary to drive authentic growth, improve student experiences, and communicate their school’s community impact in profound, data-driven ways that standard standardized testing completely misses.
takeaways: Be able to identify areas they could leverage outside support
Reflect on how to restructure past partnership opportunities
Understand through an example the tools and systems that might be created as a result of such partnerships
